hero.lead: DiscVault 26 can be installed and used today. It brings a new desktop-first library, richer detail pages, PostgreSQL foundations, plugins, migration tools, and better admin controls for collectors who want to keep their physical media catalog self-hosted.
hero.point2: Poster, list, and detail library views
hero.point5: RBAC, invite-only login, and admin tools
hero.point6: Planned DiscVault 26 features remain on the roadmap
screenshots.h2: Preview DiscVault 26, then compare it with the current desktop and mobile app.
features.h2: Built for people with real shelves, real stacks, and real editions.
f1.title: Scan and enrich
f1.body: Use your camera to scan barcodes, then automatically pull movie metadata and cover art.
f2.title: Filter and search
f2.body: Search by title, genre, or director and filter by format in a clean collection view.
f3.title: Backup & restore
f3.body: Create and download full backups of your collection, and restore them anytime with a single click.
f4.title: Works offline
f4.body: Add DiscVault to your homescreen and use it offline — browse and manage your collection without an internet connection.
f5.title: Safe by design
f5.body: Run self-hosted, control your own data path, and keep your collection data completely private.
f6.title: MCP ready
f6.body: Connect AI assistants to your library through DiscVault’s MCP endpoint for automation and search.
f7.title: User management
f7.body: Add multiple users with their own accounts and permissions to share your DiscVault instance.
f8.title: Groups
f8.body: Organize your collection into custom groups and let multiple users collaborate on shared lists.
f9.title: Passkey recovery
f9.body: Securely recover your account using passkeys — no passwords or emails required.
f10.title: MemberGroups
f10.body: Any user can create their own group and invite others by username. Invited users receive a notification and can choose to join or decline. Separate from admin-managed groups — fully user-driven collaboration.
f11.title: Watchlist & watch history
f11.body: Mark titles as watched, track when you last viewed them, and keep a personal watchlist for discs you want to get to next.
f12.title: Multiple languages
f12.body: DiscVault now supports nine languages: English, Dutch, French, German, Spanish, Portuguese, Italian, Swedish, Norwegian, Finnish, and Danish.
f13.title: Invite-only registration
f13.body: Disable open registration and require admin-issued invites. New users receive a one-time password to register their passkey — no sign-up form needed.
f14.title: Push notifications
f14.body: Group members receive a push notification on their device whenever a new disc is added to a shared group — works on any device with DiscVault installed as a PWA.
f15.title: User-scoped MCP access
f15.body: Generate a personal API key in your profile and use it to connect your AI assistant to your own collection, watchlist, and watch history — without ever exposing data from other users.
soon.h2: Previously announced features are still on the DiscVault 26 roadmap.
s1.title: Import your existing collection
s1.body: Already tracking your discs in a spreadsheet or Letterboxd? Import a CSV or Letterboxd export and have your full collection in DiscVault without scanning everything from scratch.
s2.title: Collection statistics
s2.body: Get a dashboard overview of your collection — disc count by format, genre breakdown, total runtime, and growth over time.
s3.title: Custom tags & lists
s3.body: Add freeform tags to any title — “lent out”, “wishlist”, “gift” — and build personal lists that go beyond automatic groups.
s4.title: Loan tracker
s4.body: Track which discs you’ve lent out and to whom. See all outstanding loans at a glance and add a return reminder so nothing goes missing.
s5.title: Webhooks & notifications
s5.body: Send a webhook event when a disc is added, a backup completes, or a loan is overdue — ready to connect with Home Assistant, ntfy, or any automation tool.
s6.title: Wishlist
s6.body: Keep track of discs you want to add to your collection — special editions you’re hunting for, or titles you’ve ordered but haven’t received yet. Move them to your collection with one tap once they arrive.
s_edition.title: Edition & version tracking
s_edition.body: Record which edition of a disc you own — Steelbook, Director’s Cut, Limited Edition, or standard release. Essential for collectors who own multiple versions of the same title.
s_region.title: Region indicator
s_region.body: Log the region of any disc — A/B/C for Blu-ray, regions 1–6 for DVD. Useful for import collectors and anyone with multiple players.
s_picker.title: “What to watch tonight” picker
s_picker.body: Set a few filters — genre, runtime, mood — and DiscVault picks a random title from your own shelf. Stop scrolling streaming services when the answer is already on your wall.
s_rating.title: Personal rating
s_rating.body: Give any title your own star rating or score, separate from the IMDb score. Filter and sort by what you think is great — not just what the crowd says.
s_sort.title: Sort by IMDb / TMDb score
s_sort.body: The metadata is already there — use it. Sort your collection by rating to instantly surface the best titles on your shelf.
s_public.title: Public profile page
s_public.body: Optionally share a read-only view of your collection with anyone via a link — no account required on their end.
s_watchreq.title: Watchlist requests
s_watchreq.body: Friends in a shared group can flag a title they want to watch. The owner gets a notification and can make the disc available.
s_plex.title: Compare with Plex / Jellyfin
s_plex.body: See which physical discs you also have digitally in Plex or Jellyfin — and which titles you only own physically but haven’t ripped yet.
s_letterboxd.title: Letterboxd sync
s_letterboxd.body: Automatically sync your watch history and ratings with Letterboxd, so your collection manager and film diary always stay in step.
s_shelflabel.title: Shelf label printing
s_shelflabel.body: Print tidy shelf labels for your discs, boxes, and shelves — with title, format, and a scannable code — so every title is easy to find and put back in the right place.
s_location.title: Shelf & location tracking
s_location.body: Record where each disc physically lives — shelf, box, or room. Never dig through the whole collection again when you know exactly where a title is stored.
s_condition.title: Condition & sealed status
s_condition.body: Note the condition of the case and disc, and flag titles that are still sealed. Handy for collectors who keep mint copies separate from their watch copies.
s_value.title: Collection value
s_value.body: Record what you paid and an estimated current value per title, and see the total worth of your collection at a glance — useful for insurance and tracking rare editions.
s_dupes.title: Duplicate detection
s_dupes.body: Get a heads-up when you scan or add a disc you already own, so you avoid accidental double-buys — with an override for collectors who deliberately keep multiple copies.
ios.h2: A native DiscVault app for iPhone and iPad — beta soon on TestFlight.
ios.native.title: Built natively for iOS
ios.native.body: A native iPhone and iPad app is on the way. A public beta is coming soon through TestFlight — more info to follow.
ios.standalone.title: Works standalone
ios.standalone.body: The app runs completely on its own — no server required. Manage your collection right on your device without the DiscVault backend.
ios.connect.title: Or connect to your backend
ios.connect.body: Already self-hosting? Connect the native app to your own self-hosted DiscVault 26 backend to keep everything in sync.
s_labels.title: Shelf label printing
s_labels.body: Generate printable QR or barcode labels for your shelves and boxes, so you can scan straight to the right spot and find any title in seconds.
s_pricealert.title: Price & deal alerts
s_pricealert.body: Get notified when a disc on your wishlist drops in price or goes on sale, so you never miss a good deal on the editions you’re chasing.
s_cloudbackup.title: Cloud & scheduled backups
s_cloudbackup.body: Automatically back up your collection on a schedule to S3, Nextcloud, or any WebDAV storage — set it once and never worry about losing your data.
install.h2: Install DiscVault 26, or stay on the Legacy release.
install.stable.title: DiscVault Legacy
install.stable.body: DiscVault Legacy will receive no further updates and reaches End of Life on October 1, 2026. After that date, new installs/downloads are no longer possible and the legacy Docker image will be removed.
install.beta.title: v26 image
install.beta.body: Install DiscVault 26 with the v26 image. This is the current DiscVault 26 release.
install.unraid.title: Unraid template
community.h2: Need help or want to follow updates?
f16.title: MovieVault metadata enrichment
f16.body: Opt in to enrich releases and box sets with community-sourced metadata. Corrections you add are contributed back to MovieVault, while existing DiscVault workflows stay unchanged.
faq.lead: This page is being prepared as the home for installation notes, MovieVault beta details, language support, and common troubleshooting answers.
faq.sections.h2: Practical answers for installing, updating, and signing in.
faq.install.title: Installation & updates
faq.install.body: Deployment notes for Unraid, Docker, stable releases, beta builds, backups, and updates.
faq.movievault.title: MovieVault beta
faq.movievault.body: Opt-in metadata enrichment, community contributions, privacy expectations, and beta limitations.
faq.languages.title: Languages
faq.languages.body: Supported interface languages, translation coverage, and how language selection is saved.
faq.support.title: Support & community
faq.support.body: Where to ask questions, report issues, follow releases, and join the DiscVault Discord.
faq.install.p1: DiscVault is designed to run self-hosted. The easiest setup is the all-in-one Docker container, which includes the web app and backend in one image. On Unraid, use the Community Applications template and keep your database and uploaded assets on persistent storage.
faq.install.p2: To install DiscVault 26, use the v26 image tag. To stay on the old Legacy version, use the legacy tag instead of latest.
faq.install.before.title: Before you update
faq.install.before.1: Create a backup from the admin tools or copy the persistent data directory while the container is stopped.
faq.install.before.2: Check the release notes when moving between beta and stable builds.
faq.install.before.3: Keep your environment variables and volume mappings unchanged unless the release notes say otherwise.
faq.install.update.title: Updating
faq.install.update.body: Pull the newer image, recreate the container with the same volumes and settings, then open DiscVault and confirm that your collection, users, passkeys, backups, and settings are still visible. If anything looks wrong, stop the container and restore the backup before continuing.
faq.passkeys.title: Passkeys versus passwords
faq.passkeys.p1: DiscVault uses passkeys so you do not need to create, remember, reuse, or share a password. A passkey is a cryptographic key pair: the private key stays on your device or in your trusted credential manager, while DiscVault only receives the public key.
faq.passkeys.p2: That makes passkeys safer than passwords. There is no password database for an attacker to steal, nothing useful to type into a fake login page, and every passkey is unique to the site where it was created. Signing in is also easier because you approve it with your device unlock method, such as Face ID, Touch ID, fingerprint, Windows Hello, or a device PIN.
faq.passkeys.why.title: Why they are safer and easier
faq.passkeys.why.1: Phishing resistant: the browser and operating system only use the passkey for the correct website.
faq.passkeys.why.2: No shared secret: DiscVault stores a public key, not a reusable password.
faq.passkeys.why.3: Unique per service: a passkey created for DiscVault cannot be reused somewhere else.
faq.passkeys.why.4: Fast sign-in: unlock your device instead of typing a password.
faq.passkeys.requirements.title: Minimum requirements
faq.passkeys.req.windows: Windows: use a current browser with passkey support and Windows Hello. Windows 11 version 22H2 or newer is recommended for native passkey management; Windows Hello needs at least a PIN, with face or fingerprint optional.
faq.passkeys.req.macos: macOS: macOS Ventura 13 or newer with iCloud Keychain enabled, or another passkey-capable credential manager/browser.
faq.passkeys.req.ios: iPhone and iPad: iOS 16 or iPadOS 16 or newer with iCloud Keychain and two-factor authentication enabled.
faq.passkeys.req.android: Android: Android 9 or newer with a passkey-capable credential provider such as Google Password Manager, plus a screen lock.
faq.passkeys.recovery: Tip: register more than one passkey where possible, for example your phone and your desktop device. That gives you a recovery path if one device is replaced or lost.
v26.h2: A new generation of DiscVault, already usable while the roadmap keeps moving.
v26.lead: DiscVault 26 is the next foundation for the project. It keeps the self-hosted collector focus, but modernizes the app shell, database model, admin area, plugin runtime, and migration path.
v26.f1.title: New library experience
v26.f1.body: Browse posters, switch to list or detail views, filter by format, jump into containers and box sets, and keep the interface fast on large collections.
v26.f2.title: Richer movie and container pages
v26.f2.body: Detail pages now make room for backdrops, posters, videos, metadata, collection membership, watched state, and box-set contents.
v26.f3.title: PostgreSQL and migration assistant
v26.f3.body: DiscVault 26 uses a PostgreSQL-backed model and includes a guided path to inspect and import existing DiscVault data while keeping media files in place.
v26.f4.title: Plugin-centered platform
v26.f4.body: Manage metadata sources, receivers, import sources, digital media sources, plugin packages, health checks, and background plugin jobs from the admin area.
v26.f5.title: Personal lists and watch history
v26.f5.body: Keep a personal watchlist and watched history that follows your account across devices, with poster, list, and detail layouts.
v26.f6.title: Admin, RBAC, API and MCP
v26.f6.body: DiscVault 26 adds invite-only flows, owner/admin tools, Basic and Advanced RBAC, role simulation, audit views, API access, and MCP-ready integrations.
install.note.title: Before updating
install.note.body: Create a backup first, keep your persistent data and volume mappings, and use the migration guide before moving an existing library to DiscVault 26.
migration.h2: Move to DiscVault 26 in a few guided steps
migration.lead: The migration wizard checks your current install, helps you authenticate with your passkey, shows what will be moved, and keeps progress visible until DiscVault 26 is ready to launch.
migration.step1.title: Confirm your current install
migration.step1.body: Start from the existing DiscVault environment and let the wizard detect the source before anything changes.
migration.step2.title: Review and start safely
migration.step2.body: Authenticate, choose the migration scope, and get a final ready screen before the migration begins.
migration.step3.title: Follow every step
migration.step3.body: Progress, completion, and the DiscVault 26 launch screen make the upgrade path clear from start to finish.
cap.migration.start: Start the migration from your existing DiscVault installation.
cap.migration.passkey: Authenticate with your passkey before changes are made.
cap.migration.review: Review what the wizard found in the current install.
cap.migration.ready: Confirm the final ready screen before starting.
cap.migration.progress: Watch progress while data and settings are moved.
cap.migration.finished: See the completion screen when migration is done.
cap.migration.launch: Launch DiscVault 26 from the finished flow.
screenshots.h2.v26mobile: Preview DiscVault 26 across desktop and mobile.
faq.legacy.title: Staying on the old version
faq.legacy.p1: If you are not ready to move to DiscVault 26 yet, you can stay on the previous DiscVault generation by using the legacy Docker image tag.
faq.legacy.p2: Use ghcr.io/helmerzNL/DiscVault:legacy instead of latest or beta in your Docker, Docker Compose, or Unraid template configuration. Keep your existing volume mappings, environment variables, ports, and persistent data path unchanged.
faq.legacy.steps.title: Recommended steps
faq.legacy.steps.1: Create a backup before changing the image tag.
faq.legacy.steps.2: Change only the image tag to legacy.
faq.legacy.steps.3: Pull the image, recreate the container, and confirm that your collection opens normally.
faq.legacy.note: The legacy image is meant for people who want to keep running the old version. New DiscVault 26 features, migration improvements, and future interface updates are added to the v26 image instead.
faq.upn.title: Why passkeys need a domain name (FQDN)
faq.upn.p1: Passkeys use the WebAuthn standard, which binds each passkey to the exact web address where it was created. That address must be a real domain name — a fully qualified domain name (FQDN) such as discvault.example.com — served over HTTPS. A bare IP address like http://192.168.1.50:6080 does not work for passkeys.
faq.upn.p2: When you open DiscVault, the browser ties every passkey to the relying party ID (RP_ID), which is the hostname shown in the address bar. The browser only offers a passkey when that hostname matches, and it refuses to create or use passkeys on a bare IP address or over plain HTTP. If you reach DiscVault by IP, the passkey step fails or never appears.
faq.upn.why.title: Why a domain name is required
faq.upn.why.1: Phishing protection: WebAuthn locks each passkey to one domain name, and an IP address cannot be validated as a domain, so browsers refuse to use it.
faq.upn.why.2: Secure context: browsers only allow passkeys over HTTPS or on localhost, and a trusted HTTPS certificate is issued for a domain name, not for an internal IP.
faq.upn.why.3: Stable identity: if your server IP changes, passkeys bound to that IP would break, while a domain name keeps working.
faq.upn.setup.title: How to set it up
faq.upn.setup.1: Point a domain or subdomain such as discvault.yourdomain.com at your server and put it behind HTTPS, usually through a reverse proxy.
faq.upn.setup.2: Set RP_ID to that hostname without scheme or port, and set RP_ORIGINS to the full HTTPS URL, for example https://discvault.yourdomain.com.
faq.upn.setup.3: Open DiscVault through the domain name from now on, then create your passkey. The localhost address still works on the server itself for first-time setup.
faq.upn.note: Tip: choose the domain name before creating passkeys. Passkeys are tied to the hostname, so changing it later means registering your passkeys again.
faq.collectors.title: Collectors Mode and multiple editions
faq.collectors.p1: Collectors Mode is an optional setting for serious collectors. It is turned off by default, and you enable it in the app settings. While it is off, your library shows every title as its own separate entry.
faq.collectors.p2: Turning it on unlocks grouping features that keep a large collection tidy. Most importantly, it lets DiscVault collapse different editions of the same film — for example a Blu-ray and a 4K Steelbook of the same movie — into a single library card, so you see one cover instead of several near-identical rows.
faq.collectors.unlocks.title: What Collectors Mode unlocks
faq.collectors.unlocks.1: Merge multiple editions: different owned editions of the same film are grouped behind one card, which you can tap to see every edition you own.
faq.collectors.unlocks.2: Container cards: box-sets, vaults and collections appear as a single card instead of showing all of their titles loose in the library.
faq.collectors.unlocks.3: Optional badges on those cards, such as the format (for example 4K) and the number of titles inside.
faq.collectors.editions.title: How editions are grouped
faq.collectors.editions.1: Editions are matched automatically using the most reliable identifier available: the catalogue (MovieVault) id first, then the IMDb id, and finally the title and release year.
faq.collectors.editions.2: Each edition keeps its own details, such as “Steelbook” or “Director’s Cut”. The card itself shows the edition you added most recently.
faq.collectors.note: Collectors Mode is completely optional. If you prefer to see every disc on its own, simply leave it off, or turn it back off at any time.
faq.containers.title: Box-sets, vaults and collections: what is the difference?
faq.containers.p1: DiscVault gives you three ways to group titles together. In the library they all look similar — a single container card — but they represent different things.
faq.containers.boxset.title: Box-set
faq.containers.boxset.body: A box-set is a physical retail package with its own barcode, such as “Back to the Future: The Complete Trilogy”. When you scan the outer barcode, DiscVault adds the box-set together with the films inside it, and you can deselect anything that was detected by mistake before saving. Each film is still stored as its own title, linked to the box-set.
faq.containers.vault.title: Vault
faq.containers.vault.body: A vault is a grouping you create yourself, usually to match a real shelf, storage box or location. It is not tied to a barcode or a retail product — it is simply your own way of organising discs. You choose which films belong in it.
faq.containers.collection.title: Collection
faq.containers.collection.body: A collection is a thematic grouping, such as a favourite director or studio. Unlike a box-set or a vault, a collection can also contain other containers — box-sets, vaults and even other collections — so you can nest groupings inside each other. Its film count and year range are calculated across everything nested inside.
faq.containers.compare.title: Quick comparison
faq.containers.compare.1: Box-set: a real product you own, added by scanning its barcode; it contains films.
faq.containers.compare.2: Vault: your own shelf or location grouping; it contains films and has no barcode.
faq.containers.compare.3: Collection: a thematic grouping that can also nest box-sets, vaults and other collections.
faq.containers.note: These container cards appear when Collectors Mode is on. With Collectors Mode off, the individual titles inside them are shown separately in your library.
